event_description: Yes. Hertz Global Holdings had a well-publicized accounting scandal beginning in 2014. The company disclosed material weaknesses in internal controls and announced it would restate its financial statements for fiscal years 2011, 2012, and 2013 (and revise Q1 2014 results), due to errors including improper capitalization of nonfleet vehicle costs, misstatements related to allowances for non-vehicle obligations/uncollectible amounts, and other accrual/reserve errors. This delayed multiple periodic SEC filings (10-Ks/10-Qs) for over a year. As part of the fallout, CEO Mark Frissora resigned in September 2014, and there were related departures/changes among finance executives. The SEC subsequently opened a formal investigation into Hertz's accounting practices. In December 2018, the SEC announced a settled enforcement action, with Hertz agreeing to pay an $16 million civil penalty for reporting inaccurate adjusted pre-tax income figures in earnings releases and other filings between 2011 and 2013. This is separate from Hertz's later 2020 Chapter 11 bankruptcy (unrelated to this accounting matter, driven by COVID-19 impact on rental demand).
